items.content:

items.title: From Selma to now: Are US voting rights being rolled back? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Sixty years after civil rights protesters were beaten on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, thousands are marching again in Selma - this time over new electoral maps and a changing legal landscape. Critics warn hard-won protections are being eroded. We speak to leading scholars Gloria Browne-Marshall and Gerald Horne about what’s at stake.

00:00 Are US voting rights being rolled back?
01:56 Gloria J. Browne-Marshall, John Jay College
04:02 Gerald Horne, University of Houston

items.title: Ebola outbreak spreads in DR Congo: Dozens dead as rare strain raises alarm | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The World Health Organization has declared an international emergency. Much is still unknown about the latest Ebola outbreak in Central Africa. Officials say 88 people have died, with hundreds more suspected cases in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. Doctors Without Borders says it is preparing a large-scale response. Dozens of people have died in a new Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo’s Ituri province, with cases likely spreading weeks before the official announcement. The highly contagious Bundibugyo strain — which has no vaccine — has already reached the capital Kinshasa and neighboring Uganda. Limited testing suggests the true scale may be far larger, as conflict, displacement and poor access make containment increasingly difficult, experts warn.

00:00 WHO declares international emergency over Ebola
01:11 What is Ebola, and how does it spread?
01:38 What is the WHO’s response to the outbreak?
02:44 Science journalist Kai Kupferschmidt explains why Ebola keeps breaking out
03:50 Why contact tracing is important
05:02 How close are we to a pandemic?
05:50 “Ebola is not the kind of pathogen I’d worry about spreading in Berlin or New York”
06:32 Why is the Bundibugyo strain so hard to stop?

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Ukraine launches 600 drones on Russia | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>More than 600 Ukrainian drones struck overnight across 14 Russian regions in one of the largest ever attacks since the war began. Four people were killed, including an Indian citizen. 

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said Kyiv's attack on Russia was "entirely justified." It came after Russia launched more than 1,500 drones and dozens of missiles at Ukrainian targets over the past week. On May 14, 24 people, including three children, were killed when a missile struck an apartment block in Kyiv. 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Italian PM visits victims of car ramming in hospital | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni and President Sergio Mattarella visited a hospital in northern Italy the day after a driver with a history of mental health problems ran over a group of pedestrians. Eight people were injured, four of them seriously. One victim needed to have both of her legs amputated.

items.title: Several dead near Moscow in one of Ukraine's biggest-ever drone attacks on Russia | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>One of Ukraine's largest drone attacks on Russia leaves several dead near Moscow. The strikes hit an oil refinery and two oil-pumping stations in the Moscow region, according to the Ukrainian security service. The barrage comes just days after a massive Russian attack on the Ukrainian capital killed at least 24 people.

00:00 Russian Authorities report four deaths after Ukrainian drone attacks
00:49 Military analyst Marina Miron
02:14 It’s "logical" for both sides to claim that civilians were targeted
04:10 Both warring factions increasingly use unmanned systems
05:42 Ukraine and Russia are now in a war of attrition

items.title: Ukraine launches one of its biggest-ever drone strikes on Russia | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Ukraine has launched one of its largest drone strikes on Moscow and other regions in Russia since the beginning of the war. The attacks come alongside intense strikes on Russian oil facilities and tightening controls within the country. What does this signal about the direction of the war - and about pressure on the Kremlin? We’re joined by Jenny Mathers, expert in Russian politics and security at Aberystwyth University.

0:00 Intro
1:45 What’s the significance of these strikes?
4:06 Strikes on Russian Oil Refineries
6:20 How will Putin Respond?
7:35 Ukraine’s Drone Capabilities Explained
9:18 Russia vs Ukraine: Drone Production Power

items.title: Bolivian police clash with protesters blockading La Paz | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Protests in Bolivia that began over wages and fuel prices have escalated into nationwide unrest and calls for President Rodrigo Paz to resign. Police and military forces have clashed with demonstrators blockading major roads in and out of the capital La Paz. The blockades have fueled a shortage of basic goods in the city.

items.title: How USAID cuts could result in higher HIV transmission | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Kenya has made major progress in reducing HIV infections. Now that progress is under threat. Free condom supplies, once widely available through international aid, are shrinking after funding cuts, including from the United States. Health experts warn this could reverse years of progress, especially for people who cannot afford alternatives. 

00:00 Aid Cuts Hit Kenya: Free Condoms Running Out 
04:20 DW speaks with Stanley Ngara King, Kenya's 'King of Condoms,

items.author: DW News
items.content:

items.title: Former poachers in Nigeria find redemption as forest rangers | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>In Nigeria’s remaining rainforest reserves, wildlife is under pressure from logging, hunting, and economic hardship. But one former poacher is now part of the solution. This report follows rangers working to protect the forest, showing how conservation can also create new opportunities for local communities. DW West Africa correspondent Amaka Okoye reports from Okumu National Park in Nigeria.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/
